```xml
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='notify/consult ICU or higher-level provider and activate transfer/escalation protocol' objects='ICU consult request, transfer protocol' id='activity22.1'/>
          <activity role='ED/bed management' action='arrange and execute transfer logistics (bed assignment, transport, handover) to higher level of care' objects='ICU bed assignment, transport resources, handover note' id='activity22.2'/>
          <activity role='ED/bed management' action='activate pathway/mechanism to transfer patient to a higher level provider' objects='ICU consult, transfer protocol' id='activity22'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='requires inpatient admission with normal nurse-to-patient staffing (average intensity)' id='branch20'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='admit to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='admission order, bed request' id='activity23'/>
          <activity role='ED/inpatient team' action='monitor for deterioration/escalation triggers during ED boarding/early admission period' objects='vitals, telemetry, labs, nursing assessment' id='activity24.1'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
            <branch condition='deterioration/escalation triggers met (e.g., worsening hypoxemia, hemodynamic instability, refractory congestion, malignant arrhythmia)' id='branch21'>
              <activity role='ED/bed management' action='escalate/consult higher-level provider and initiate transfer pathway if required' objects='escalation protocol, consult request' id='activity24.2'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='no deterioration triggers met' id='branch22'>
              <activity role='ED/inpatient team' action='continue routine inpatient admission flow without escalation at this time' objects='admission workflow, monitoring plan' id='activity24.3'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
          <activity role='ED/bed management' action='use pathway/mechanism to transfer/escalate to a higher level provider if needed' objects='escalation protocol, consult request' id='activity24'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
</process>
```

[2026-09-02 23:33:25 INFO] flask app.py did not start for online log
[2026-09-02 23:33:25 INFO] **[Seminar Conclusion]**:

 Problem: In the exclusive gateway with id='exclusivegateway2', the discharge follow-up activities are duplicated and out of order: `activity20.2` (“schedule follow-up…”) and `activity20` (“arrange follow-up…”) describe the same step, and `activity20` is redundant after `activity20.2`.

Suggested modification: In branch id='branch17' of `exclusivegateway2`, remove `activity20` and keep a single follow-up scheduling activity after sending the discharge summary.

Reason: Duplicate activities create ambiguity and can lead to inconsistent execution/documentation; the process should have one clear step to arrange/schedule follow-up.

Modified process text (only showing the corrected branch17 section in context; all other parts unchanged):
```xml
<process>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='perform triage and confirm suspected acute heart failure presentation' objects='triage assessment, presenting symptoms, initial exam' id='activity1.1'/>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='collect required standardized risk-stratification variables (history, comorbidities, meds, directives) and enter into tool' objects='risk stratification tool, patient history, medication list, advance directives' id='activity1.2'/>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='apply standardized risk assessment/stratification tool consistently (multi-condition tool including HF)' objects='risk stratification tool, patient presentation data' id='activity1'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='ED nurse/clinician' action='obtain serum creatinine and electrolyte levels' objects='blood sample, lab requisition' id='activity2'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch2'>
      <activity role='ED nurse/clinician' action='obtain troponin measurements' objects='blood sample, lab requisition' id='activity3'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch3'>
      <activity role='ED nurse/clinician' action='obtain complete blood count' objects='blood sample, lab requisition' id='activity4'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch4'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='perform electrocardiogram' objects='ECG machine, patient' id='activity5'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch5'>
      <activity role='ED clinician/radiology' action='perform chest x-ray examination' objects='CXR order, imaging equipment' id='activity6'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch9'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='measure heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation frequently until patient is stabilized' objects='vital signs monitor, pulse oximeter' id='activity9'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='no recent echocardiogram available' id='branch7'>
      <activity role='cardiology/echo lab' action='perform echocardiogram' objects='echocardiogram order, ultrasound equipment' id='activity7'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='recent echocardiogram available' id='branch8'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='review recent echocardiogram results' objects='prior echocardiogram report' id='activity8'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='administer/continue IV loop diuretic (e.g., furosemide) per protocol and document dose/time' objects='diuretic order, medication, administration record' id='activity10.1'/>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='assess responsiveness to diuresis (urine output, weight change, symptoms, congestion signs) and document' objects='urine output, weight, symptoms, diuretic dose/response' id='activity10.2'/>
  <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='respiratory distress present' id='branch10'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: respiratory distress' objects='clinical assessment' id='activity11'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='hypoxemia present' id='branch11'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: hypoxemia' objects='oxygen saturation/ABG results' id='activity12'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='pulmonary edema severe' id='branch12'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: severity of pulmonary edema' objects='exam findings, chest x-ray results' id='activity13'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='poor response to furosemide in ED' id='branch13'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: poor response to furosemide administered in ED' objects='diuretic response data' id='activity14'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='hemodynamic compromise present' id='branch14'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: hemodynamic compromise' objects='BP, perfusion, lactate/clinical signs' id='activity15'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='significant arrhythmias present' id='branch15'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: significant arrhythmias' objects='ECG/telemetry findings' id='activity16'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='troponin positive' id='branch16'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: positive troponin' objects='troponin results' id='activity17'/>
    </branch>
  </inclusiveGateway>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='compile EHMRG inputs from history, vitals, ECG, and labs; verify completeness' objects='EHMRG variables, vitals, ECG interpretation, lab results' id='activity18.1'/>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='calculate and document acute heart failure risk score (e.g., EHMRG) and predicted 7-day mortality risk' objects='EHMRG calculator, patient variables, chart note' id='activity18.2'/>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
    <branch condition='EHMRG quintiles 1–2 AND responded to initial ED treatment AND no other considerations (e.g., advanced directives, severe dementia, impact of admission on life expectancy)' id='branch17'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='treat in ED or outpatient setting and discharge home without inpatient admission (low intensity)' objects='discharge plan, patient education' id='activity19'/>
      <activity role='ED staff' action='prepare and send discharge summary/hand-off to primary care provider' objects='discharge summary, medication list, test results' id='activity20.1'/>
      <activity role='ED staff' action='schedule follow-up visit with primary care provider within days and provide appointment details to patient' objects='appointment request, contact information, patient instructions' id='activity20.2'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='EHMRG quintiles 3–5 OR other considerations requiring admission' id='branch18'>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='requires ventilation (noninvasive or invasive) and/or ICU-level care (high intensity)' id='branch19'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='initiate ventilation as indicated (noninvasive or invasive) and admit to ICU with higher nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='ventilation equipment, ICU bed request' id='activity21'/>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='notify/consult ICU or higher-level provider and activate transfer/escalation protocol' objects='ICU consult request, transfer protocol' id='activity22.1'/>
          <activity role='ED/bed management' action='arrange and execute transfer logistics (bed assignment, transport, handover) to higher level of care' objects='ICU bed assignment, transport resources, handover note' id='activity22.2'/>
          <activity role='ED/bed management' action='activate pathway/mechanism to transfer patient to a higher level provider' objects='ICU consult, transfer protocol' id='activity22'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='requires inpatient admission with normal nurse-to-patient staffing (average intensity)' id='branch20'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='admit to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='admission order, bed request' id='activity23'/>
          <activity role='ED/inpatient team' action='monitor for deterioration/escalation triggers during ED boarding/early admission period' objects='vitals, telemetry, labs, nursing assessment' id='activity24.1'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
            <branch condition='deterioration/escalation triggers met (e.g., worsening hypoxemia, hemodynamic instability, refractory congestion, malignant arrhythmia)' id='branch21'>
              <activity role='ED/bed management' action='escalate/consult higher-level provider and initiate transfer pathway if required' objects='escalation protocol, consult request' id='activity24.2'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='no deterioration triggers met' id='branch22'>
              <activity role='ED/inpatient team' action='continue routine inpatient admission flow without escalation at this time' objects='admission workflow, monitoring plan' id='activity24.3'/>
```

| **task_prompt** | Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above). Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure Module 1: Risk Stratification Module 1 Recommended Practices 1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals. 1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis Initial investigations should include: • serum creatinine and electrolyte levels • troponin measurements • complete blood count • electrocardiogram • chest x-ray examination • an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available • frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized 1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED. Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing. High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing. High-risk markers include: • respiratory distress • hypoxemia • severity of pulmonary edema • poor response to furosemide administered in ED • hemodynamic compromise • significant arrhythmias • positive troponin 1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score). As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital. Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence. Module 1 Implementation Considerations General Considerations for Risk Stratification • Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department. • All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider |

Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure Module 1: Risk Stratification Module 1 Recommended Practices 1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals. 1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis Initial investigations should include: • serum creatinine and electrolyte levels • troponin measurements • complete blood count • electrocardiogram • chest x-ray examination • an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available • frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized 1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED. Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing. High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing. High-risk markers include: • respiratory distress • hypoxemia • severity of pulmonary edema • poor response to furosemide administered in ED • hemodynamic compromise • significant arrhythmias • positive troponin 1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score). As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital. Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence. Module 1 Implementation Considerations General Considerations for Risk Stratification • Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department. • All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider |

[2026-09-02 23:33:25 INFO] Process design reviewer: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider.
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider
process text: 
<process>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='perform triage and confirm suspected acute heart failure presentation' objects='triage assessment, presenting symptoms, initial exam' id='activity1.1'/>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='collect required standardized risk-stratification variables (history, comorbidities, meds, directives) and enter into tool' objects='risk stratification tool, patient history, medication list, advance directives' id='activity1.2'/>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='apply standardized risk assessment/stratification tool consistently (multi-condition tool including HF)' objects='risk stratification tool, patient presentation data' id='activity1'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='ED nurse/clinician' action='obtain serum creatinine and electrolyte levels' objects='blood sample, lab requisition' id='activity2'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch2'>
      <activity role='ED nurse/clinician' action='obtain troponin measurements' objects='blood sample, lab requisition' id='activity3'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch3'>
      <activity role='ED nurse/clinician' action='obtain complete blood count' objects='blood sample, lab requisition' id='activity4'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch4'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='perform electrocardiogram' objects='ECG machine, patient' id='activity5'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch5'>
      <activity role='ED clinician/radiology' action='perform chest x-ray examination' objects='CXR order, imaging equipment' id='activity6'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch9'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='measure heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation frequently until patient is stabilized' objects='vital signs monitor, pulse oximeter' id='activity9'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='no recent echocardiogram available' id='branch7'>
      <activity role='cardiology/echo lab' action='perform echocardiogram' objects='echocardiogram order, ultrasound equipment' id='activity7'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='recent echocardiogram available' id='branch8'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='review recent echocardiogram results' objects='prior echocardiogram report' id='activity8'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='administer/continue IV loop diuretic (e.g., furosemide) per protocol and document dose/time' objects='diuretic order, medication, administration record' id='activity10.1'/>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='assess responsiveness to diuresis (urine output, weight change, symptoms, congestion signs) and document' objects='urine output, weight, symptoms, diuretic dose/response' id='activity10.2'/>
  <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='respiratory distress present' id='branch10'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: respiratory distress' objects='clinical assessment' id='activity11'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='hypoxemia present' id='branch11'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: hypoxemia' objects='oxygen saturation/ABG results' id='activity12'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='pulmonary edema severe' id='branch12'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: severity of pulmonary edema' objects='exam findings, chest x-ray results' id='activity13'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='poor response to furosemide in ED' id='branch13'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: poor response to furosemide administered in ED' objects='diuretic response data' id='activity14'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='hemodynamic compromise present' id='branch14'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: hemodynamic compromise' objects='BP, perfusion, lactate/clinical signs' id='activity15'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='significant arrhythmias present' id='branch15'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: significant arrhythmias' objects='ECG/telemetry findings' id='activity16'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='troponin positive' id='branch16'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: positive troponin' objects='troponin results' id='activity17'/>
    </branch>
  </inclusiveGateway>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='compile EHMRG inputs from history, vitals, ECG, and labs; verify completeness' objects='EHMRG variables, vitals, ECG interpretation, lab results' id='activity18.1'/>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='calculate and document acute heart failure risk score (e.g., EHMRG) and predicted 7-day mortality risk' objects='EHMRG calculator, patient variables, chart note' id='activity18.2'/>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
    <branch condition='EHMRG quintiles 1–2 AND responded to initial ED treatment AND no other considerations (e.g., advanced directives, severe dementia, impact of admission on life expectancy)' id='branch17'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='treat in ED or outpatient setting and discharge home without inpatient admission (low intensity)' objects='discharge plan, patient education' id='activity19'/>

    </branch>

      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='requires ventilation (noninvasive or invasive) and/or ICU-level care (high intensity)' id='branch19'>

            <branch condition='deterioration/escalation triggers met (e.g., worsening hypoxemia, hemodynamic instability, refractory congestion, malignant arrhythmia)' id='branch21'>
              <activity role='ED/bed management' action='escalate/consult higher-level provider and initiate transfer pathway if required' objects='escalation protocol, consult request' id='activity24.2'/>

          </exclusiveGateway>
          <activity role='ED/bed management' action='use pathway/mechanism to transfer/escalate to a higher level provider if needed' objects='escalation protocol, consult request' id='activity24'/>

suggestion:
Problem: In the exclusive gateway with id='exclusivegateway2', the discharge follow-up activities are duplicated and out of order: `activity20.2` (“schedule follow-up…”) and `activity20` (“arrange follow-up…”) describe the same step, and `activity20` is redundant after `activity20.2`.

Suggested modification: In branch id='branch17' of `exclusivegateway2`, remove `activity20` and keep a single follow-up scheduling activity after sending the discharge summary.

Reason: Duplicate activities create ambiguity and can lead to inconsistent execution/documentation; the process should have one clear step to arrange/schedule follow-up.

You need to modify the process text according to the suggestions and output it.
Be careful not to change the format:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

[2026-09-02 23:33:34 INFO] Process design reviewer: **Process design reviewer<->Team leader on : AddAPI, turn 0**

[You're a team leader. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
Your main responsibilities will include guiding the process design expert through process generation, process refinement, and so on.
Here is a new customer's task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider.
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

        <branch condition='no recent echocardiogram available' id='branch3a'>
          <activity role='cardiology/echo lab' action='perform echocardiogram' objects='echocardiography' id='activity8'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='recent echocardiogram available' id='branch3b'>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='review most recent echocardiogram results' objects='prior echocardiogram report' id='activity9'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>

  <activity role='ED physician' action='assess responsiveness to diuresis (including response to furosemide administered in ED)' objects='diuresis response data' id='activity11'/>
  <activity role='ED physician' action='administer or adjust IV diuretic per protocol and document timing/dose' objects='diuretic order/protocol' id='activity11_1'/>
  <activity role='ED nurse' action='reassess diuresis response (urine output, symptom relief, repeat vitals) after diuretic' objects='I&O record/clinical reassessment' id='activity11_2'/>

  <activity role='ED physician' action='screen for high-risk markers (respiratory distress, hypoxemia, severity of pulmonary edema, hemodynamic compromise, significant arrhythmias, positive troponin, poor response to furosemide in ED)' objects='clinical findings and test results' id='activity12'/>
  <activity role='ED physician' action='calculate acute heart failure risk score (e.g., EHMRG) to predict 7-day mortality risk' objects='EHMRG inputs and score' id='activity13'/>

  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
    <branch condition='high intensity required (ventilation and/or ICU with higher nurse-to-patient staffing) OR high-risk markers present' id='branch5'>
      <activity role='ED physician' action='activate pathway to transfer patient to higher level provider (ICU/critical care)' objects='transfer protocol' id='activity14'/>
      <activity role='ED physician' action='notify receiving ICU/critical care team and request acceptance' objects='consult/notification request' id='activity14_1'/>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='prepare patient for transfer and complete standardized handover package' objects='handover checklist/transfer documents' id='activity14_2'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='noninvasive ventilation required' id='branch6'>
          <activity role='ED/respiratory therapy' action='initiate noninvasive ventilation' objects='NIV equipment' id='activity15'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='invasive ventilation required' id='branch7'>
          <activity role='ED/critical care team' action='initiate invasive ventilation' objects='airway equipment/ventilator' id='activity16'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <activity role='bed management/critical care' action='admit to intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='ICU bed' id='activity17'/>
    </branch>

    <branch condition='not high intensity; EHMRG quintiles 3-5 (higher-risk) or other clinical considerations favor admission' id='branch8'>
      <activity role='ED physician' action='admit patient to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing (average intensity)' objects='admission orders' id='activity18'/>
    </branch>

    <branch condition='EHMRG quintiles 1-2 (low-risk) AND responded to initial ED treatment AND no other considerations (e.g., advanced directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy)' id='branch9'>
      <activity role='ED physician' action='treat in ED or outpatient setting and discharge home without inpatient admission (low intensity)' objects='discharge plan' id='activity19'/>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='provide discharge instructions and self-management education (worsening symptoms, daily weights, when to return)' objects='patient instructions/education materials' id='activity19_1'/>
      <activity role='ED physician/pharmacist' action='reconcile and confirm discharge medications and dosing' objects='medication list/prescriptions' id='activity19_2'/>
      <activity role='ED staff' action='arrange follow-up visit with primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED' objects='follow-up appointment details' id='activity20'/>
      <activity role='ED clerk/scheduling' action='book follow-up appointment and document date/time/contact' objects='scheduling system' id='activity20_1'/>
      <activity role='ED physician/clerical' action='send discharge summary and key test results to primary care provider' objects='discharge summary/diagnostic results' id='activity20_2'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
</process>)</API>

[2026-09-02 23:33:51 INFO] Process design expert: **Process design expert<->Process design reviewer on : ProcessReview, turn 0**

[You're a process design reviewer. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include give suggestion to the process.
Here is a new customer's task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider.
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

  <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='respiratory distress present' id='branch10'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: respiratory distress' objects='clinical assessment' id='activity11'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='hypoxemia present' id='branch11'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: hypoxemia' objects='oxygen saturation/ABG results' id='activity12'/>

      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: severity of pulmonary edema' objects='exam findings, chest x-ray results' id='activity13'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='poor response to furosemide in ED' id='branch13'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: poor response to furosemide administered in ED' objects='diuretic response data' id='activity14'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='hemodynamic compromise present' id='branch14'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: hemodynamic compromise' objects='BP, perfusion, lactate/clinical signs' id='activity15'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='significant arrhythmias present' id='branch15'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: significant arrhythmias' objects='ECG/telemetry findings' id='activity16'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='troponin positive' id='branch16'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: positive troponin' objects='troponin results' id='activity17'/>
    </branch>
  </inclusiveGateway>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='compile EHMRG inputs from history, vitals, ECG, and labs; verify completeness' objects='EHMRG variables, vitals, ECG interpretation, lab results' id='activity18.1'/>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='calculate and document acute heart failure risk score (e.g., EHMRG) and predicted 7-day mortality risk' objects='EHMRG calculator, patient variables, chart note' id='activity18.2'/>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
    <branch condition='EHMRG quintiles 1–2 AND responded to initial ED treatment AND no other considerations (e.g., advanced directives, severe dementia, impact of admission on life expectancy)' id='branch17'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='treat in ED or outpatient setting and discharge home without inpatient admission (low intensity)' objects='discharge plan, patient education' id='activity19'/>
      <activity role='ED staff' action='prepare and send discharge summary/hand-off to primary care provider' objects='discharge summary, medication list, test results' id='activity20.1'/>
      <activity role='ED staff' action='schedule follow-up visit with primary care provider within days and provide appointment details to patient' objects='appointment request, contact information, patient instructions' id='activity20.2'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='EHMRG quintiles 3–5 OR other considerations requiring admission' id='branch18'>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='requires ventilation (noninvasive or invasive) and/or ICU-level care (high intensity)' id='branch19'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='initiate ventilation as indicated (noninvasive or invasive) and admit to ICU with higher nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='ventilation equipment, ICU bed request' id='activity21'/>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='notify/consult ICU or higher-level provider and activate transfer/escalation protocol' objects='ICU consult request, transfer protocol' id='activity22.1'/>
          <activity role='ED/bed management' action='arrange and execute transfer logistics (bed assignment, transport, handover) to higher level of care' objects='ICU bed assignment, transport resources, handover note' id='activity22.2'/>
          <activity role='ED/bed management' action='activate pathway/mechanism to transfer patient to a higher level provider' objects='ICU consult, transfer protocol' id='activity22'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='requires inpatient admission with normal nurse-to-patient staffing (average intensity)' id='branch20'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='admit to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='admission order, bed request' id='activity23'/>
          <activity role='ED/inpatient team' action='monitor for deterioration/escalation triggers during ED boarding/early admission period' objects='vitals, telemetry, labs, nursing assessment' id='activity24.1'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
            <branch condition='deterioration/escalation triggers met (e.g., worsening hypoxemia, hemodynamic instability, refractory congestion, malignant arrhythmia)' id='branch21'>
              <activity role='ED/bed management' action='escalate/consult higher-level provider and initiate transfer pathway if required' objects='escalation protocol, consult request' id='activity24.2'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='no deterioration triggers met' id='branch22'>
              <activity role='ED/inpatient team' action='continue routine inpatient admission flow without escalation at this time' objects='admission workflow, monitoring plan' id='activity24.3'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
          <activity role='ED/bed management' action='use pathway/mechanism to transfer/escalate to a higher level provider if needed' objects='escalation protocol, consult request' id='activity24'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
</process>

[2026-09-02 23:33:51 INFO] Team leader: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design reviewer. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include give suggestion to the process.
Here is a new customer's task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider.
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

</process>

    </branch>

    </branch>

      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='requires ventilation (noninvasive or invasive) and/or ICU-level care (high intensity)' id='branch19'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='initiate ventilation as indicated (noninvasive or invasive) and admit to ICU with higher nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='ventilation equipment, ICU bed request' id='activity21'/>

[You're a team leader. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
Your main responsibilities will include guiding the process design expert through process generation, process refinement, and so on.
Here is a new customer's task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider.
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

Please check the format of the process text:
<API>check(
<process>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='perform triage and confirm suspected acute heart failure presentation' objects='triage assessment, presenting symptoms, initial exam' id='activity1.1'/>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='collect required standardized risk-stratification variables (history, comorbidities, meds, directives) and enter into tool' objects='risk stratification tool, patient history, medication list, advance directives' id='activity1.2'/>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='apply standardized risk assessment/stratification tool consistently (multi-condition tool including HF)' objects='risk stratification tool, patient presentation data' id='activity1'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='ED nurse/clinician' action='obtain serum creatinine and electrolyte levels' objects='blood sample, lab requisition' id='activity2'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch2'>
      <activity role='ED nurse/clinician' action='obtain troponin measurements' objects='blood sample, lab requisition' id='activity3'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch3'>
      <activity role='ED nurse/clinician' action='obtain complete blood count' objects='blood sample, lab requisition' id='activity4'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch4'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='perform electrocardiogram' objects='ECG machine, patient' id='activity5'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch5'>
      <activity role='ED clinician/radiology' action='perform chest x-ray examination' objects='CXR order, imaging equipment' id='activity6'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch9'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='measure heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation frequently until patient is stabilized' objects='vital signs monitor, pulse oximeter' id='activity9'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='no recent echocardiogram available' id='branch7'>
      <activity role='cardiology/echo lab' action='perform echocardiogram' objects='echocardiogram order, ultrasound equipment' id='activity7'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='recent echocardiogram available' id='branch8'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='review recent echocardiogram results' objects='prior echocardiogram report' id='activity8'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='administer/continue IV loop diuretic (e.g., furosemide) per protocol and document dose/time' objects='diuretic order, medication, administration record' id='activity10.1'/>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='assess responsiveness to diuresis (urine output, weight change, symptoms, congestion signs) and document' objects='urine output, weight, symptoms, diuretic dose/response' id='activity10.2'/>
  <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='respiratory distress present' id='branch10'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: respiratory distress' objects='clinical assessment' id='activity11'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='hypoxemia present' id='branch11'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: hypoxemia' objects='oxygen saturation/ABG results' id='activity12'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='pulmonary edema severe' id='branch12'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: severity of pulmonary edema' objects='exam findings, chest x-ray results' id='activity13'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='poor response to furosemide in ED' id='branch13'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: poor response to furosemide administered in ED' objects='diuretic response data' id='activity14'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='hemodynamic compromise present' id='branch14'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: hemodynamic compromise' objects='BP, perfusion, lactate/clinical signs' id='activity15'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='significant arrhythmias present' id='branch15'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: significant arrhythmias' objects='ECG/telemetry findings' id='activity16'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='troponin positive' id='branch16'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='document high-risk marker: positive troponin' objects='troponin results' id='activity17'/>
    </branch>
  </inclusiveGateway>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='compile EHMRG inputs from history, vitals, ECG, and labs; verify completeness' objects='EHMRG variables, vitals, ECG interpretation, lab results' id='activity18.1'/>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='calculate and document acute heart failure risk score (e.g., EHMRG) and predicted 7-day mortality risk' objects='EHMRG calculator, patient variables, chart note' id='activity18.2'/>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
    <branch condition='EHMRG quintiles 1–2 AND responded to initial ED treatment AND no other considerations (e.g., advanced directives, severe dementia, impact of admission on life expectancy)' id='branch17'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='treat in ED or outpatient setting and discharge home without inpatient admission (low intensity)' objects='discharge plan, patient education' id='activity19'/>
      <activity role='ED staff' action='prepare and send discharge summary/hand-off to primary care provider' objects='discharge summary, medication list, test results' id='activity20.1'/>
      <activity role='ED staff' action='schedule follow-up visit with primary care provider within days and provide appointment details to patient' objects='appointment request, contact information, patient instructions' id='activity20.2'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='EHMRG quintiles 3–5 OR other considerations requiring admission' id='branch18'>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='requires ventilation (noninvasive or invasive) and/or ICU-level care (high intensity)' id='branch19'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='initiate ventilation as indicated (noninvasive or invasive) and admit to ICU with higher nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='ventilation equipment, ICU bed request' id='activity21'/>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='notify/consult ICU or higher-level provider and activate transfer/escalation protocol' objects='ICU consult request, transfer protocol' id='activity22.1'/>
          <activity role='ED/bed management' action='arrange and execute transfer logistics (bed assignment, transport, handover) to higher level of care' objects='ICU bed assignment, transport resources, handover note' id='activity22.2'/>
          <activity role='ED/bed management' action='activate pathway/mechanism to transfer patient to a higher level provider' objects='ICU consult, transfer protocol' id='activity22'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='requires inpatient admission with normal nurse-to-patient staffing (average intensity)' id='branch20'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='admit to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='admission order, bed request' id='activity23'/>
          <activity role='ED/inpatient team' action='monitor for deterioration/escalation triggers during ED boarding/early admission period' objects='vitals, telemetry, labs, nursing assessment' id='activity24.1'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
            <branch condition='deterioration/escalation triggers met (e.g., worsening hypoxemia, hemodynamic instability, refractory congestion, malignant arrhythmia)' id='branch21'>
              <activity role='ED/bed management' action='escalate/consult higher-level provider and initiate transfer pathway if required' objects='escalation protocol, consult request' id='activity24.2'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='no deterioration triggers met' id='branch22'>
              <activity role='ED/inpatient team' action='continue routine inpatient admission flow without escalation at this time' objects='admission workflow, monitoring plan' id='activity24.3'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
          <activity role='ED/bed management' action='use pathway/mechanism to transfer/escalate to a higher level provider if needed' objects='escalation protocol, consult request' id='activity24'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
</process>)</API>
